zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Margarett Chaucer于大约1308年出生在英国諾廷加姆爾时，她的父亲Thomas Chancer46岁，母亲Matersey44岁。她于从1328年1月9日到1329年1月8日与Sir George Monbourcher在英国英格兰諾廷加姆爾结婚。他们至少是1个儿子和4个女儿的父母。

nameMeanings.name: Chaucer
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: Norman, English: occupational name from Old French chaucier ‘maker of chausses’, an agent derivative of Old French chauces ‘breeches, pantaloons, hose’. In 1484 these were ‘chauces of yron or legge harneys’ ( OED ). Middle English chauce was a general term for anything worn on the feet: boots, shoes, hose, or leggings. As Baldwin le Chaucer (1307 London Letter Books B) was of Cordwanerstrete, the early chaucer was probably a worker in leather, a maker of leather breeches, boots, etc.

variants.name: London Letter Books
meanings: 1  Scottish: locative name from Broats in Kirkpatrick-Fleming (Dumfriess).

2 English: nickname from Middle English bro(u)che ‘pointed weapon or instrument; clasp, brooch, pin’, perhaps for one who made or sold such items. Compare Ralph Brocher, 1222 in Feet of Fines (Suffolk); John Brocher, Roger le Brochere, 1281 in London Letter Books B. Compare William Bruchemaker, 1381 in Yorks Poll Tax and William ploghbrocher, 1281 in Fransson (Lincs), probably a maker of ploughshares.

variants.name: Causer
meanings: English (West Midlands): probably a variant of Chauser, an occupational name for a maker of leggings or other apparel for the legs or feet, from an agent derivative probably of a northern variant of Old French cauc(i)er, chaucier ‘maker of hose, shoemaker’, although Cauc(i)er, the Norman and Picard form of the name, has not yet been noted in English medieval records. Alternatively, perhaps a variant of Cosier, an occupational name from Middle English cosere ‘horse dealer’.

variants.name: OED
meanings: English: unexplained. Perhaps from the legal term lawed, passive participle of a verb meaning ‘to mutilate the toes (of a hunting dog to prevent its being used for poaching)’, therefore perhaps ‘lame’ ( OED , law, verb, 3., recorded from 1534); or perhaps from Middle English laude ‘praise’.

nameMeanings.name: Margarett
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

variants.name: Margaret
meanings: An extremely common given name from the Middle Ages onwards, derived via Old French Marguerite and Latin Margarita from Greek Margarītēs, from margaron ‘pearl’, a word ultimately of Hebrew origin. The name was always understood to mean ‘pearl’ throughout the Middle Ages. The first St Margaret was martyred at Antioch in Pisidia during the persecution instigated by the Emperor Diocletian in the early 4th century. However, there is some doubt about her name, as the same saint is venerated in the Orthodox Church as Marina . There were several other saintly bearers of the name, including St Margaret of Scotland ( d. 1093 ), wife of King Malcolm Canmore and daughter of Edmund Ironside of England. It was also the name of the wife of Henry VI of England, Margaret of Anjou ( 1430–82 ), and of Margaret Tudor ( 1489–1541 ), sister of Henry VIII , who married James IV of Scotland and ruled as regent there after his death. See also Margery , Marjorie .

variants.name: Margareta
meanings: From the Latin form of Greek Margarītēs. See Margaret .

historicEvents.title: Famine Strikes Europe
historicEvents.description: A famine caused by cold winters and wet summers across Europe caused millions to die due to starvation. Desperation from hunger caused high levels of crime, stampedes in bread lines in London, infanticide, and even cannibalism throughout Europe.

historicEvents.title: First War of Scottish Independence
historicEvents.description: The First War of Scottish Independence began after England invaded Scotland in 1296. The Treaty of Edinburgh was signed in 1328 which declared peace between the two kingdoms and brought the war to an end.

historicEvents.title: Wycliffe's Bible
historicEvents.description: John Wycliffe, English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, completed a translation of the Bible from the Latin Vulgate into Middle English. This translation is known as Wycliffe's Bible and was completed around 1382.

en.lifeSummary: When Margarett Chaucer was born about 1308, in Nottinghamshire, England, her father, Thomas Chancer, was 46 and her mother, Matersey, was 44. She married Sir George Monbourcher from 9 January 1328 to 8 January 1329, in Nottinghamshire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 1 son and 4 daughters.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Margarett Chaucer est née environ 1308, à Nottinghamshire, Angleterre, son père, Thomas Chancer, avait 46 ans et sa mère, Matersey, avait 44 ans. Elle a épousé Sir George Monbourcher du 9 janvier 1328 au 8 janvier 1329, à Nottinghamshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils et 4 filles.

de.lifeSummary: Als Margarett Chaucer um 1308 in Nottinghamshire, England geboren wurde, war ihr Vater Thomas Chancer 46 und ihre Mutter Matersey 44. Sie hat von 9. Januar 1328 bis 8. Januar 1329 in Nottinghamshire, England, United Kingdom Sir George Monbourcher geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 1 Sohn und 4 Töchter.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Margarett Chaucer è nata il circa 1308 nel Nottinghamshire, England, sua padre, Thomas Chancer, aveva 46 anni e sua madre, Matersey, aveva 44 anni. Lei sposato Sir George Monbourcher da 9 gennaio 1328 a 8 gennaio 1329, nel Nottinghamshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito. Erano genitori di almeno 1 figlio e 4 figlie.
